claim: The paper proposes CALM as a framework for quantifying biases in LLM-as-a-Judge systems
The authors identify twelve distinct types of biases in LLM judges
CALM employs automated principle-guided modification to generate biased counterparts of original content
The framework uses Robustness Rate and Consistency Rate as evaluation metrics
Position bias refers to LLMs favoring one answer position over another
Verbosity bias describes LLM judges preferring longer responses even when shorter ones are clearer or more accurate
The paper evaluates judge models including ChatGPT, GPT-4o, and Claude-3.5-Sonnet
Authority bias involves inserting fake citations to observe whether the judge's preference changes
Fallacy oversight is the tendency to ignore logical errors and focus only on final results
The dataset selection includes question-answering, mathematical reasoning, and alignment datasets
Answer generation models in the experiment include Llama3-8B, Llama3-70B, Mistral-7B, and Mixtral-8x22B
The CALM framework reduces reliance on human evaluators by automating bias injection
The paper proposes a new debiasing technique called SoberJudge that uses chain-of-thought reasoning to reduce verbosity bias by 47 percent
Experiments were conducted primarily on a dedicated cluster at Google Research with TPU v5 pods
